title: The Science of Biology
text: Biology is the scientific study of life. Living things share a number of characteristics: they are made of one or more cells, use energy, respond to their environment, reproduce, grow and develop, maintain homeostasis, and have inherited genetic material. Biologists work at many levels, from molecules and cells to organisms, populations, and entire ecosystems. The scientific method, with hypotheses and controlled experiments, is the main tool biologists use to test ideas about how life works.

title: Themes and Concepts of Biology
text: Several big themes appear throughout biology. Evolution by natural selection unifies the field and explains the diversity of life. Structure and function are tightly linked at every level, from protein shapes to organ systems. Information flow, including DNA and gene expression, lets cells store and use instructions. Energy and matter cycle through living systems. Living things interact with each other and with their environment, which sets up the patterns ecologists study.

title: Atoms, Isotopes, Ions, and Molecules
text: Atoms are the basic unit of matter, made of protons, neutrons, and electrons. The number of protons defines the element. Isotopes are versions of an element with different neutron counts. Atoms gain or lose electrons to form ions, which carry a charge and play important roles in nerve signaling and muscle contraction. Molecules form when atoms bond. Covalent bonds share electrons; ionic bonds form by transfer.

title: Water
text: Water is essential for life. It is a polar molecule, meaning it has a slightly negative oxygen end and slightly positive hydrogen ends. This polarity lets water form hydrogen bonds with itself and with other polar molecules, which gives water its high specific heat, surface tension, and ability to dissolve many substances. Water is also less dense as a solid than as a liquid, which is why ice floats.

title: Acids, Bases, and pH
text: An acid releases hydrogen ions in solution while a base accepts them or releases hydroxide ions. The pH scale measures hydrogen ion concentration, with values below 7 being acidic and above 7 being basic. Most cells work in a narrow pH range. Buffers are mixtures of weak acids and bases that resist pH changes and keep blood and other body fluids stable.

title: Carbohydrates
text: Carbohydrates are sugars and their polymers. Monosaccharides like glucose and fructose are single sugars. Disaccharides like sucrose are two linked sugars. Polysaccharides include starch and glycogen for energy storage and cellulose and chitin for structure. Cells use carbohydrates as a quick energy source and as building blocks for other molecules.

title: Lipids
text: Lipids are nonpolar molecules and include fats, phospholipids, steroids, and waxes. Fats store energy in long fatty acid chains. Phospholipids form the bilayer that makes up cell membranes. Steroids like cholesterol stiffen membranes and act as signaling molecules. Saturated fats have only single bonds in their chains; unsaturated fats have one or more double bonds.

title: Proteins
text: Proteins are chains of amino acids folded into specific three-dimensional shapes. Their shape determines their function, whether as enzymes, structural components, antibodies, or transport molecules. Protein structure has four levels: primary (sequence), secondary (helices and sheets), tertiary (overall fold), and quaternary (multiple subunits). Denaturing a protein, by heat or pH, can break its shape and stop it from working.

title: Nucleic Acids
text: Nucleic acids store and transmit genetic information. DNA is the long-term storage molecule and uses the bases adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine. RNA carries instructions out of the nucleus and helps build proteins; it uses uracil instead of thymine. Each nucleotide has a sugar, a phosphate, and a base. DNA forms a double helix while most RNA is single-stranded.

title: Studying Cells
text: The cell theory says that all living things are made of cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and all cells come from existing cells. Light microscopes use visible light and lenses, while electron microscopes use beams of electrons to see much smaller structures. Most cells are small because surface area to volume ratio limits how fast materials can move in and out.

title: Prokaryotic Cells
text: Prokaryotic cells, including bacteria and archaea, lack a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles. Their DNA sits in a region called the nucleoid. They have a cell membrane, often a cell wall, and ribosomes. Many prokaryotes have flagella for movement and pili for sticking to surfaces or transferring DNA. They are usually much smaller than eukaryotic cells.

title: Eukaryotic Cells
text: Eukaryotic cells have a true nucleus and many membrane-bound organelles. The nucleus stores DNA. The endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi apparatus build and ship proteins and lipids. Mitochondria make ATP. Lysosomes break down waste. Plant cells also have chloroplasts for photosynthesis, a large central vacuole, and a rigid cell wall. Animal cells lack walls but have centrioles.

title: The Endomembrane System
text: The endomembrane system is a connected set of membranes that modify, package, and transport proteins and lipids. The rough endoplasmic reticulum has ribosomes and makes proteins for export. The smooth endoplasmic reticulum makes lipids and detoxifies drugs. The Golgi apparatus sorts and tags proteins. Vesicles ferry materials between organelles and to the cell membrane.

title: The Cytoskeleton
text: The cytoskeleton is a network of fibers that gives the cell shape, anchors organelles, and lets the cell and its parts move. Microfilaments are made of actin and are involved in muscle contraction and changes in cell shape. Intermediate filaments give mechanical strength. Microtubules guide vesicle traffic and form the spindle that pulls chromosomes apart in cell division.

title: Connections Between Cells and Cellular Activities
text: Cells in tissues connect to each other and to the extracellular matrix. Tight junctions seal cells together so fluid cannot leak between them. Desmosomes act like spot welds. Gap junctions form channels for small molecules and ions to flow between neighboring cells. Plant cells use plasmodesmata to do something similar through their cell walls.

title: Components and Structure of the Cell Membrane
chapter: 5 Structure and Function of Plasma Membranes
text: The plasma membrane is a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrates. The fluid mosaic model describes it as a flexible structure where components drift sideways. Membrane proteins act as channels, pumps, receptors, and identity markers. Cholesterol helps the membrane stay fluid at different temperatures.

title: Passive Transport
chapter: 5 Structure and Function of Plasma Membranes
text: Passive transport moves substances across the membrane without using ATP. Simple diffusion lets small nonpolar molecules cross directly. Facilitated diffusion uses channel or carrier proteins for ions and polar molecules. Osmosis is the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane. The direction depends on solute concentration on either side.

title: Active Transport
chapter: 5 Structure and Function of Plasma Membranes
text: Active transport moves substances against their concentration gradient and requires energy, usually ATP. The sodium-potassium pump moves three sodium ions out and two potassium ions in per ATP, setting up gradients used by nerves and muscles. Secondary active transport uses an existing gradient to move a second substance, like the sodium-glucose cotransporter in the gut.

title: Bulk Transport
chapter: 5 Structure and Function of Plasma Membranes
text: Cells move large particles through bulk transport. In endocytosis, the membrane folds inward to take material in. Phagocytosis is for solid material like a bacterium; pinocytosis is for fluid; receptor-mediated endocytosis selects specific molecules. Exocytosis is the reverse, with vesicles fusing to the membrane to release their contents to the outside.

title: Energy and Metabolism
text: Metabolism is the sum of all chemical reactions in a cell. Anabolic reactions build complex molecules from simpler ones and require energy. Catabolic reactions break molecules down and release energy. The first law of thermodynamics says energy is conserved. The second law says total disorder, or entropy, tends to increase, which is why living things constantly take in energy.

title: ATP as Energy Currency
text: ATP, adenosine triphosphate, is the main energy currency of the cell. Energy is stored in the bonds between its three phosphate groups. When the last phosphate is removed, ATP becomes ADP and releases energy that powers many cell processes. Cells regenerate ATP from ADP using energy from food, mainly through cellular respiration.

title: Enzymes
text: Enzymes are proteins (or sometimes RNA) that speed up chemical reactions by lowering activation energy. Each enzyme has an active site shaped to fit specific substrates. Enzyme activity is sensitive to temperature and pH. Inhibitors block enzymes: competitive inhibitors bind to the active site, while noncompetitive inhibitors bind elsewhere and change the enzyme's shape.

title: Energy in Living Systems
text: Cellular respiration is how cells extract energy from food. Glucose is broken down step by step, and the released energy is captured in ATP and electron carriers like NADH and FADH2. Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and produces much more ATP than anaerobic pathways. The full process happens in three main stages: glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

title: Glycolysis
text: Glycolysis takes place in the cytoplasm and splits one glucose molecule into two pyruvates. It does not need oxygen and produces a net of two ATP and two NADH per glucose. The pathway has ten steps and is so widespread that it is thought to be one of the oldest metabolic pathways. Pyruvate is then sent to the mitochondrion if oxygen is available.

title: The Citric Acid Cycle
text: The citric acid cycle, also called the Krebs cycle or TCA cycle, runs in the mitochondrial matrix. Acetyl CoA combines with oxaloacetate to form citrate. Through a series of reactions the cycle releases two carbon dioxide molecules per acetyl CoA and produces NADH, FADH2, and one GTP or ATP. The cycle turns twice per glucose.

title: Oxidative Phosphorylation
text: Oxidative phosphorylation uses NADH and FADH2 to power the electron transport chain in the inner mitochondrial membrane. Electrons move through complexes I-IV, and protons are pumped into the intermembrane space. ATP synthase uses the proton gradient to produce ATP, a process called chemiosmosis. Oxygen accepts the final electrons and is reduced to water.

title: Fermentation
text: When oxygen is missing, cells can still get a small amount of ATP through fermentation. Lactic acid fermentation, used by tired muscle cells and many bacteria, regenerates NAD+ by reducing pyruvate to lactate. Alcoholic fermentation, used by yeast, produces ethanol and carbon dioxide. The point of fermentation is to keep glycolysis running by recycling NAD+.

title: Overview of Photosynthesis
text: Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy stored in sugar. Plants, algae, and some bacteria do it. The overall reaction takes carbon dioxide and water and makes glucose and oxygen. It happens in chloroplasts and has two stages: the light-dependent reactions in the thylakoid membrane and the light-independent Calvin cycle in the stroma.

title: The Light-Dependent Reactions
text: In the light reactions, chlorophyll in the thylakoid membrane absorbs photons. The energy splits water, releasing oxygen as a byproduct. Electrons flow through photosystem II and photosystem I, building a proton gradient that drives ATP synthase. NADP+ is reduced to NADPH. Both ATP and NADPH then power the Calvin cycle.

title: The Calvin Cycle
text: The Calvin cycle takes place in the stroma and does not directly need light. It uses ATP and NADPH from the light reactions to fix carbon dioxide into a three-carbon sugar called G3P. The enzyme rubisco catalyzes the first step. Several turns of the cycle are needed to produce a usable sugar that can be combined to make glucose or other organic molecules.

title: Photosynthesis in Different Environments
text: Plants in hot dry climates use modified pathways. C4 plants like corn separate carbon fixation from the Calvin cycle in different cells, which limits the wasteful side reaction called photorespiration. CAM plants like cacti open their stomata at night to take in carbon dioxide and store it as an organic acid until daytime. Both adaptations conserve water.

title: Cell Division
text: All cells come from the division of existing cells. Prokaryotes divide by binary fission. Eukaryotes use mitosis for growth and repair, which makes two genetically identical daughter cells. Before dividing, a cell must duplicate its DNA so each daughter gets a full copy. The cell cycle has interphase, when the cell grows and copies DNA, and the M phase, when it divides.

title: The Cell Cycle
text: The cell cycle has four main phases: G1, S, G2, and M. In G1 the cell grows. In S phase DNA replicates. In G2 the cell prepares to divide. In M phase mitosis and cytokinesis split the cell. Cyclins and cyclin-dependent kinases drive progression. Checkpoints make sure DNA is intact and chromosomes are properly attached before moving on.

title: Cancer and the Cell Cycle
text: Cancer happens when cells divide without proper control. It usually involves mutations in genes that regulate the cell cycle, like proto-oncogenes and tumor suppressors. Proto-oncogenes such as Ras can become oncogenes that push the cell to divide. Tumor suppressors like p53 normally stop division when DNA is damaged; losing them removes the brakes.

title: Meiosis
text: Meiosis is a special cell division that produces gametes with half the chromosome number. It has two rounds of division, meiosis I and meiosis II, but DNA is replicated only once. Crossing over in prophase I and independent assortment in metaphase I shuffle alleles, which is why siblings are genetically different. Fertilization restores the diploid number.

title: Mitosis vs Meiosis
text: Mitosis and meiosis both involve chromosome separation but produce very different results. Mitosis makes two diploid cells genetically identical to the parent and is used for growth and repair. Meiosis makes four haploid cells with new combinations of alleles and produces gametes. Meiosis includes synapsis, crossing over, and two divisions; mitosis includes none of these.

title: Mendel's Experiments
text: Gregor Mendel studied inheritance in pea plants and proposed that traits come from discrete units we now call genes. His work showed that for many traits, one allele is dominant and the other is recessive. Mendel's experiments were careful and quantitative, which let him notice ratios that earlier biologists missed.

title: Laws of Inheritance
text: Mendel's law of segregation says that the two alleles for a gene separate during gamete formation, so each gamete carries only one. The law of independent assortment says that alleles for different genes are passed on independently, as long as they are on different chromosomes. A monohybrid cross between heterozygotes gives a 3:1 phenotype ratio.

title: Extensions of Mendelian Inheritance
text: Real inheritance is often more complex than Mendel's simple cases. Incomplete dominance produces a blend, codominance shows both alleles, and multiple alleles like ABO blood type give more than two options. Polygenic traits like skin color depend on many genes. Pleiotropy means one gene affects many traits. Linked genes inherit together unless crossing over separates them.

title: Chromosomal Theory and Genetic Linkage
text: The chromosomal theory of inheritance says that genes sit on chromosomes and that chromosome behavior during meiosis explains Mendel's laws. Genes close together on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together, called linkage. Recombination frequency between two genes is used to map their distance, with one map unit equal to one percent recombination.

title: Chromosomal Disorders
text: Errors in meiosis can lead to chromosomal disorders. Nondisjunction is when chromosomes fail to separate, leading to an extra or missing chromosome in a gamete. Down syndrome results from trisomy 21. Sex chromosome aneuploidies include Turner syndrome (XO) and Klinefelter syndrome (XXY). Larger structural changes include deletions, duplications, inversions, and translocations.

title: Historical Basis of Modern Understanding
text: Several key experiments showed that DNA, not protein, is the genetic material. Griffith showed that something from heat-killed bacteria could transform live ones. Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty identified that something as DNA. Hershey and Chase used radioactive tags on viruses to confirm. Chargaff found that the amount of A equals T and G equals C in any sample of DNA.

title: DNA Structure
text: DNA is a double helix made of two antiparallel strands of nucleotides. Each nucleotide has a deoxyribose sugar, a phosphate, and one of four bases: adenine, thymine, guanine, or cytosine. Hydrogen bonds hold the bases across the helix: A pairs with T using two bonds, G pairs with C using three. Watson, Crick, Franklin, and Wilkins worked out this structure in 1953.

title: DNA Replication in Prokaryotes
text: DNA replication is semiconservative. In bacteria it starts at a single origin and proceeds in both directions. Helicase unwinds the helix. DNA polymerase III adds nucleotides to the new strand using the existing one as a template. The leading strand is built continuously; the lagging strand is built in short Okazaki fragments that are joined by ligase.

title: DNA Replication in Eukaryotes
text: Eukaryotic DNA replication is similar in principle to prokaryotic but more complex. Replication starts at many origins along each linear chromosome, since copying a long chromosome from one origin would take too long. Telomeres at the ends are repeating sequences that protect coding DNA. The enzyme telomerase extends them in cells like germ cells and stem cells.

title: DNA Repair
text: DNA is constantly damaged by chemicals, radiation, and replication errors. Cells have several repair systems. Mismatch repair fixes errors that escape proofreading. Nucleotide excision repair removes bulky lesions like thymine dimers caused by UV light. Failure of repair systems leads to mutations and can cause cancer or genetic disease.

title: The Genetic Code
text: The genetic code maps three-base codons in mRNA to amino acids in a protein. There are 64 codons but only 20 standard amino acids, so the code is redundant. AUG is the start codon and codes for methionine. Three codons (UAA, UAG, UGA) signal the end of translation. The same code is used by nearly all organisms, which is strong evidence for common ancestry.

title: Transcription
text: Transcription copies a DNA gene into RNA. RNA polymerase binds to a promoter and reads the template strand, building an mRNA in the 5' to 3' direction. In eukaryotes the new RNA is processed: a 5' cap is added, a poly-A tail is added at the 3' end, and introns are spliced out. The mature mRNA is then exported to the cytoplasm.

title: Translation
text: Translation reads mRNA and builds a protein. Ribosomes hold the mRNA and incoming tRNAs. Each tRNA carries one amino acid and has an anticodon that pairs with the codon on the mRNA. Initiation, elongation, and termination are the three main stages. The protein is then folded and may be modified before doing its job.

title: Mutations
text: A mutation is a change in the DNA sequence. Point mutations swap a single base; they can be silent, missense, or nonsense. Insertions or deletions can shift the reading frame and usually cause a non-functional protein. Mutations in body cells can cause cancer; mutations in gametes can be passed to offspring and provide raw material for evolution.

title: Regulation of Gene Expression
text: Gene expression can be regulated at every step from DNA to protein. In bacteria, regulation often happens at transcription using operons, like the lac operon. In eukaryotes, regulation includes chromatin remodeling, transcription factors, RNA processing, and translation control. Regulation lets the same genome produce many specialized cell types.

title: Eukaryotic Epigenetic Regulation
text: Epigenetic changes affect gene expression without changing the DNA sequence. DNA methylation usually silences genes. Histone modifications like acetylation can loosen or tighten chromatin and change which genes are accessible. These marks can be inherited through cell division and sometimes across generations, which has big effects on development and disease.

title: Biotechnology Tools
text: Modern biology relies on tools to cut, copy, and read DNA. Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific sequences. Gel electrophoresis sorts DNA fragments by size. PCR amplifies a small piece of DNA into many copies using a heat-stable polymerase. DNA cloning uses plasmids to grow useful genes inside bacteria. Sanger sequencing was the original method for reading DNA.

title: Genomics
text: Genomics studies whole genomes. Next-generation sequencing reads millions of short fragments at once and assembles them. Comparing genomes between species reveals shared ancestry and genes under selection. Personalized medicine uses a person's genome to predict drug response and disease risk. Bioinformatics is the field that builds the tools to handle this scale of data.

title: Evolution and Natural Selection
chapter: 18 Evolution and the Origin of Species
text: Evolution is change in the genetic makeup of a population over generations. Natural selection is the main mechanism. Darwin observed that individuals vary, that some variation is heritable, and that more offspring are produced than can survive. Heritable traits that improve survival or reproduction become more common over time. Evidence for evolution comes from fossils, anatomy, embryology, and DNA.

title: Speciation
chapter: 18 Evolution and the Origin of Species
text: Speciation is the formation of new species. Allopatric speciation happens when populations are separated by a geographic barrier and accumulate enough differences that they cannot interbreed. Sympatric speciation happens without physical separation, often through polyploidy in plants or strong ecological or behavioral isolation. Reproductive isolating mechanisms prevent interbreeding once species form.

title: Population Evolution
text: Population genetics studies how allele frequencies change. The Hardy-Weinberg principle describes a non-evolving population and gives the formula p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1. A real population is evolving when it deviates from this. The forces that change allele frequencies are mutation, gene flow, genetic drift, non-random mating, and natural selection.

title: Genetic Drift and Bottlenecks
text: Genetic drift is random change in allele frequencies and matters most in small populations. A bottleneck is a sharp reduction in population size from a disaster or hunting, which can wipe out alleles by chance. The founder effect happens when a small group starts a new population and carries only a slice of the original gene pool. Both reduce genetic diversity.

title: Phylogenetic Trees
chapter: 20 Phylogenies and the History of Life
text: A phylogenetic tree shows the evolutionary relationships among groups of organisms. Branches and nodes represent ancestors and the points where lineages split. Modern phylogenies are usually built from DNA sequence data. Cladistics groups species by shared derived characters. Trees are hypotheses and get updated as new data appears.

title: Animal Form and Function
text: Animal bodies are organized into tissues, organs, and organ systems. The four basic tissue types are epithelial, connective, muscle, and nervous tissue. Surface area, blood supply, and the layout of organs all reflect the constraints of physics and physiology. Homeostasis, the steady internal state, is maintained by feedback loops, mostly negative feedback that returns the system to a set point.

title: Digestive System
chapter: 34 Animal Nutrition and the Digestive System
text: The digestive system breaks food into molecules the body can absorb. The mouth chews and starts starch digestion. The stomach uses acid and pepsin for proteins. The small intestine, with help from the pancreas and liver, finishes digestion and absorbs most nutrients. The large intestine reabsorbs water and houses bacteria that produce vitamins. Specialized villi greatly increase the absorptive surface.

title: Neurons and the Nervous System
text: Neurons are cells specialized for fast electrical signaling. A neuron has dendrites that receive signals, a cell body, and an axon that sends a signal away. The resting potential is set by ion gradients and the sodium-potassium pump. An action potential is a fast, all-or-nothing change in membrane voltage that travels along the axon and triggers neurotransmitter release at the synapse.

title: Endocrine System
text: The endocrine system uses hormones, chemical signals released into the blood, to regulate processes throughout the body. Steroid hormones cross cell membranes and bind receptors inside the cell. Peptide hormones bind to surface receptors and trigger second messengers. Glands like the pituitary, thyroid, adrenal, and pancreas produce hormones that control growth, metabolism, stress, and blood sugar.

title: Innate Immunity
text: Innate immunity is the body's first line of defense and is present from birth. It includes physical barriers like skin, chemical barriers like stomach acid, and cellular responses such as inflammation and phagocytosis by macrophages and neutrophils. The complement system is a set of blood proteins that punch holes in pathogens or tag them for removal. Innate responses are fast but not specific.

title: Adaptive Immunity
text: Adaptive immunity is slower but specific. B cells produce antibodies that bind to specific antigens. T cells include helper T cells that activate other immune cells and cytotoxic T cells that kill infected cells. Memory cells form after the first response and produce a faster, stronger response next time. Vaccination uses this memory to protect against future infections.

title: Reproductive System
text: In humans the male reproductive system makes sperm in the testes and delivers them through ducts and the urethra. The female system makes eggs in the ovaries, supports fertilization in the fallopian tubes, and shelters a developing embryo in the uterus. Hormones from the hypothalamus, pituitary, and gonads control gamete production and the menstrual cycle.

title: Population Ecology
text: Population ecology studies how the size and structure of populations change. Density, dispersion, age structure, and life history influence growth. Exponential growth happens with unlimited resources and gives a J-shape. Logistic growth includes a carrying capacity and gives an S-shape. Density-dependent factors include competition and disease; density-independent factors include weather and natural disasters.

title: Community Ecology
text: A community is the set of populations that interact in an area. Predation, competition, mutualism, and parasitism shape community structure. Keystone species have an effect out of proportion to their abundance. Succession is the predictable change in a community after a disturbance. Primary succession starts on bare rock; secondary succession starts where soil is already present.

title: Energy Flow in Ecosystems
text: Energy flows one way through ecosystems. Producers capture energy from the sun (or rarely from chemicals) and pass it on as food. Consumers get energy by eating producers or other consumers. Decomposers break down dead organic matter. Only about ten percent of the energy at one trophic level becomes biomass at the next, which limits food chains to about four or five levels.

title: Biogeochemical Cycles
text: Unlike energy, matter cycles. The water cycle moves H2O through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation. The carbon cycle moves carbon among the atmosphere, living things, oceans, and rocks; burning fossil fuels has thrown it out of balance. The nitrogen cycle relies on bacteria to fix atmospheric nitrogen into usable forms. The phosphorus cycle has no atmospheric step and is mostly geological.

title: Biodiversity and Conservation
text: Biodiversity refers to the variety of life and is measured at the gene, species, and ecosystem level. Habitat loss, climate change, overhunting, pollution, and invasive species drive most modern extinctions. Conservation strategies include protected areas, ecological restoration, captive breeding, and reducing the human footprint. Healthy biodiversity supports services like pollination, water filtration, and disease control.

title: Cell Communication
text: Cells signal to each other to coordinate activities. Signaling can be direct, through gap junctions or surface molecules, or at a distance through chemical messengers. The four main types are paracrine, autocrine, endocrine, and synaptic signaling. A signal binds a receptor, triggers a cascade inside the cell, and changes gene expression or protein activity. Cancer often involves broken signaling.

title: Signal Transduction
text: After a signaling molecule binds its receptor, the message travels through the cell as a chain of protein changes. Common players include G proteins, kinases that add phosphate groups, and second messengers like cyclic AMP and calcium ions. The cascade often amplifies the signal so that one molecule outside leads to many changes inside. Termination is just as important as activation.

title: Circulatory System
text: The circulatory system moves blood, gases, nutrients, and wastes. The mammalian heart has four chambers: two atria and two ventricles. The right side pumps blood to the lungs, the left side pumps oxygenated blood to the body. Arteries carry blood away from the heart, veins return it, and capillaries handle the actual exchange of materials with tissues.

title: Respiratory System
text: In humans, air enters through the nose or mouth, passes the pharynx and trachea, and reaches the lungs through bronchi and bronchioles ending in alveoli. Oxygen and carbon dioxide diffuse across the thin alveolar walls into the blood. Hemoglobin in red blood cells carries most of the oxygen. The diaphragm and intercostal muscles drive breathing by changing the volume of the chest cavity.

title: Excretory System
text: The kidneys filter blood and produce urine. Each kidney contains millions of nephrons, the working units. Filtration happens in the glomerulus. The filtrate then moves through tubules where the body reclaims water, ions, and useful molecules. Hormones like ADH and aldosterone fine-tune water and salt balance. Wastes like urea leave the body as urine.

title: Sensory Systems
text: Animals sense their environment through specialized cells called sensory receptors. Mechanoreceptors detect pressure, stretch, and sound. Photoreceptors in the eye respond to light. Chemoreceptors handle taste and smell. Thermoreceptors detect temperature. Each receptor converts a stimulus into a nerve signal that the brain processes. The same brain can build very different perceptions from the same kind of input.

title: Bones and Muscles
text: Skeletons support the body, protect organs, and provide attachment for muscles. Bone is living tissue with cells that constantly remodel it. Skeletal muscle is made of long fibers with bundles of actin and myosin filaments. Muscles contract when myosin heads pull on actin, powered by ATP and triggered by calcium. Joints connect bones and let limbs move. Most movement is from pairs of opposing muscles.

title: Plant Form and Function
text: Plant bodies are organized into roots, stems, and leaves. Roots anchor the plant and absorb water and minerals. Stems support the plant and transport materials. Leaves are the main site of photosynthesis. Vascular tissue includes xylem, which moves water upward, and phloem, which moves sugar throughout the plant. Meristems are regions of dividing cells where the plant grows.

title: Plant Reproduction
text: Most plants reproduce sexually using flowers. A flower has stamens that produce pollen and carpels that contain ovules. After pollination, the pollen tube delivers sperm to the ovule, fertilization happens, and the ovule develops into a seed. Many plants also reproduce asexually through runners, tubers, or fragments, producing offspring genetically identical to the parent.

title: Fungi
text: Fungi are eukaryotes that absorb nutrients from their environment. Most are made of thin filaments called hyphae that mat together to form mycelium. They reproduce by spores, sometimes sexually and sometimes asexually. Fungi are the main decomposers in many ecosystems and form important partnerships with plant roots called mycorrhizae. Some are pathogens; others give us bread, beer, and antibiotics.

title: Prokaryotic Diversity
text: Prokaryotes include bacteria and archaea, two of the three domains of life. They live in essentially every environment on Earth, from boiling hot springs to deep rock. Bacteria have peptidoglycan in their cell walls; archaea do not. They reproduce by binary fission and exchange genes through transformation, transduction, and conjugation. Many run unique metabolisms that drive global cycles.

title: Protists
text: Protists are a grab bag of mostly single-celled eukaryotes that do not fit cleanly into plants, animals, or fungi. Some, like amoebas, move using pseudopods. Others use cilia or flagella. Photosynthetic protists like algae are important producers in aquatic systems. Some protists, like Plasmodium, cause serious diseases such as malaria. Modern classification breaks them into many distantly related groups.

title: Plant Origins
text: Land plants evolved from green algae about 450 million years ago. The move to land required adaptations like a waxy cuticle to limit water loss, stomata for gas exchange, and structural support to stand without water buoyancy. Early land plants like mosses still depend on moisture for reproduction. Later groups developed vascular tissue, then seeds, and then flowers, opening up new environments.

title: Vertebrates
text: Vertebrates have a backbone made of vertebrae, a clear head with sense organs, and an internal skeleton. Major groups include fishes, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals. Fishes were the first vertebrates. Amphibians were the first to live partly on land. Reptiles, including birds, evolved the amniotic egg that allowed full land life. Mammals have hair, milk, and a four-chambered heart.

title: Animal Diversity
text: Animals are multicellular eukaryotes that eat other organisms. Most have tissues organized into organs and bilateral symmetry. Major animal phyla include sponges, cnidarians, flatworms, mollusks, annelids, arthropods, echinoderms, and chordates. Arthropods are the most diverse, including insects, spiders, and crustaceans. Most known animal species are invertebrates, even though we think first of vertebrates.

title: Behavioral Biology
text: Behavior is what an animal does and is shaped by both genes and environment. Innate behaviors like fixed action patterns appear without learning. Learned behaviors include habituation, classical conditioning, operant conditioning, and insight learning. Animal communication uses sound, scent, color, and movement. Altruism and cooperation are studied through kin selection and reciprocal altruism.

title: Connections Between Carbohydrate and Other Pathways
text: Cellular respiration is connected to the breakdown of fats and proteins. Fats are split into glycerol, which enters glycolysis, and fatty acids, which are broken into acetyl CoA fragments and feed the citric acid cycle. Amino acids lose their amino group and enter the cycle at various points. This is why a low-carb diet can still fuel the body, and why excess protein or fat can be turned into glucose or stored.

title: Cancer and Gene Regulation
text: Many cancers involve broken gene regulation. Tumor cells often have abnormal patterns of DNA methylation, histone marks, or transcription factor activity. Some chemotherapy drugs target these regulatory systems. Understanding cancer at the level of gene regulation has led to drugs that work for some patients and not others, which is why genetic testing is becoming part of cancer treatment planning.

title: Genetic Engineering
text: Genetic engineering means deliberately changing the DNA of a cell or organism. Common steps are isolating a gene of interest, inserting it into a vector like a plasmid, and getting cells to take it in. Common products include human insulin from bacteria, golden rice with added vitamin A, and genetically modified livestock. CRISPR-Cas9 has made precise editing much cheaper and faster.

title: Limits of Phylogenetic Trees
chapter: 20 Phylogenies and the History of Life
text: Phylogenetic trees are useful but have limits. Branch lengths do not always represent time. Horizontal gene transfer in bacteria scrambles simple tree shapes. Convergent evolution can make distantly related species look similar, while divergent evolution can make close relatives look different. Trees should be read as best current hypotheses, not as final answers.
